text: The lighthouse keeper had stopped expecting visitors years before the morning the girl appeared on his rocks, salt-stiff and silent, holding a lantern that no longer burned. He had grown accustomed, by then, to the particular arithmetic of solitude: how the days subtracted themselves quietly, how the sea returned each evening like a debt it could never quite repay. He did not call out to her. Something in the way she stood—neither asking nor fleeing, simply present, as the tide is present—suggested she had come not to be rescued but to deliver something he had spent his whole stubborn life refusing to hear. He set down his cup. The light above them turned, patient, indifferent, and went on sweeping the dark.
